response: Quantum computers use qubits that can exist in superposition and become entangled, enabling certain calculations to run exponentially faster than on classical machines. Google illustrated this potential in 2019, announcing it completed a task in 200 seconds that it said would take a top classical supercomputer about 10,000 years, while IBM, IonQ, Rigetti and others pursue competing hardware platforms such as superconducting circuits, trapped ions, photonics and topological qubits. Today’s prototypes have roughly 100–1,000 physical qubits, but millions—and robust error-correction—will be required before practical systems can tackle real-world problems. If those engineering hurdles are overcome, quantum computing could transform fields from cryptography and drug discovery to financial modeling and logistics.
